title: Topic 101: The Collector's Compulsion: Why Completionists Spend More
content:
# Topic 101: The Collector's Compulsion: Why Completionists Spend More

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the fascinating, and often expensive, world of the “completionist.” We explore the psychological drivers that compel individuals to collect every virtual doodad, unlock every achievement, and generally refuse to leave any digital stone unturned. Through a rigorous, yet surprisingly hilarious, analysis of academic literature and real-world gaming phenomena, we reveal how the fear of missing out (FOMO), a Peter Pan-esque desire for immortality, and a spectrum of spending personalities from “tightwad” to “spendthrift” converge to create the perfect storm of consumer behavior. We argue that completionism is not merely a quirky hobby, but a powerful lens through which to understand the modern consumer, a creature perpetually tempted by the siren song of “just one more.”

## INTRODUCTION

It often starts innocently. A single, shiny virtual sword. A rare, elusive achievement. A limited-edition skin for a beloved character. But for a certain breed of individual, the completionist, this is not the end, but the beginning of a long, arduous, and often wallet-draining journey. The completionist is a unique specimen in the grand ecosystem of consumerism, an individual for whom the phrase “good enough” is a personal affront. They are the ones who will spend countless hours, and not-so-countless dollars, to achieve that coveted 100% completion status, a digital badge of honor that proclaims to the world, “I came, I saw, I collected everything.”

But what drives this compulsive need to complete? Why is the allure of a complete set so powerful that it can override our better judgment and our bank account balances? This paper seeks to answer these questions by exploring the psychological underpinnings of completionism. We will journey into the minds of these dedicated collectors, examining the fears, desires, and cognitive biases that shape their behavior. From the existential dread of missing out to the god-like thrill of virtual immortality, we will uncover the surprisingly deep-seated motivations behind this seemingly trivial pursuit. And, in doing so, we will shed light on a fundamental aspect of human nature: our insatiable desire for more.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

Our understanding of the completionist begins in the digital playgrounds of video games. As one scholar of the virtual realm astutely observes, the compulsion to complete is often rooted in a profound, albeit subconscious, fear of death and a more immediate, and consciously felt, fear of missing out (FOMO) [1]. Games, with their magical ability to “undo,” “restore,” and “restart,” offer a tantalizing taste of immortality, a stark contrast to the irreversible finality of real life. This virtual fountain of youth, it is argued, is a powerful draw for players, a way to experience a consequence-free existence where every mistake can be erased and every path can be explored. The completionist, in this light, is not just a collector of things, but a collector of experiences, a hoarder of alternate realities, desperately trying to outrun the specter of a single, linear existence.

This fear of a singular path is amplified by what has been termed “The Paradox of Choice” [1]. In a world of seemingly infinite options, the act of choosing one thing necessarily means forgoing another, a source of considerable anxiety for many. The completionist’s solution to this dilemma is simple, if not a bit extreme: choose everything. By collecting every item, exploring every location, and completing every quest, they effectively eliminate the possibility of having made the “wrong” choice. It is a strategy for achieving a sense of control and safety in a world, both real and virtual, that is often chaotic and unpredictable.

Of course, the desire to collect everything is not without its financial implications. Here, we turn to the psychology of spending, which conveniently categorizes consumers into three distinct archetypes: the “tightwad,” the “spendthrift,” and the “unconflicted” [2]. The tightwad, as the name suggests, experiences a sharp, emotional pain when parting with their money, while the spendthrift feels little to no such discomfort, making them far more susceptible to the siren’s call of a new, shiny object. The unconflicted, as one might guess, reside in a state of zen-like balance between these two extremes. The completionist, it would seem, is often a spendthrift in disguise, their compulsive need to collect overriding any latent tightwad tendencies. This is a dangerous combination, a perfect storm of desire and financial abandon that can lead to what can only be described as a “collector’s conflict,” a perpetual battle between the joy of acquisition and the cold, hard reality of a dwindling bank account.

## ANALYSIS

The completionist, as we have seen, is a complex creature, driven by a potent cocktail of existential dread, choice anxiety, and a predisposition for guilt-free spending. But how do these psychological factors translate into the real-world behavior of spending more? The answer lies in the clever, and some might say diabolical, ways in which game developers and marketers have learned to exploit these very human vulnerabilities.

The modern video game is a masterclass in the art of temptation. The once-simple act of buying a game and playing it to completion has been replaced by a never-ending stream of downloadable content (DLC), microtransactions, and “live service” updates. For the average player, these are optional extras, a way to enhance their gaming experience if they so choose. But for the completionist, they are non-negotiable. A new character skin, a new weapon, a new story chapter – each is a new item to be collected, a new box to be checked on their ever-expanding to-do list. The fear of an incomplete collection, of a game that is not truly “100%,” is a powerful motivator, and one that developers are all too happy to capitalize on.

Consider the “loot box,” a virtual treasure chest containing a random assortment of in-game items. For a small fee, players can purchase a key to unlock one of these boxes, with the hope of obtaining a rare and valuable item. For the completionist, the loot box is a particularly insidious invention. The random nature of the rewards means that there is no guarantee of getting the specific item they need to complete their collection. This can lead to a cycle of repeated purchases, a gambling-like behavior driven by the desperate hope that the next box will be the one that finally completes the set. It is a system that preys on the completionist’s desire for control and their aversion to uncertainty, turning their hobby into a potentially expensive and frustrating endeavor.

But it is not just about the big, flashy purchases. The completionist’s spending habits are also influenced by a more subtle, but no less powerful, psychological phenomenon: the “endowment effect.” This is the tendency for people to overvalue things that they own. For the completionist, every item in their collection, no matter how small or seemingly insignificant, becomes a part of their identity, a tangible representation of their time and effort. This makes it all the more difficult to resist the temptation to add to their collection, as each new item is not just a purchase, but an investment in their sense of self.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The world of gaming is rife with examples of completionist compulsion in action. Take, for instance, the popular online shooter *Overwatch*. The game features a vast array of cosmetic items, including character skins, emotes, and voice lines, all of which can be obtained through loot boxes. For the dedicated *Overwatch* completionist, this means spending countless hours, and potentially hundreds of dollars, to collect every last item for every single character. The sheer volume of content makes this a monumental task, yet there are players who have achieved it, their dedication a testament to the power of the collector’s impulse.

Another prime example can be found in the world of mobile gaming, specifically in the genre of “gacha” games. These games, which are particularly popular in Japan, are built around a mechanic similar to that of a capsule-toy vending machine. Players spend in-game currency, which can be purchased with real money, to “pull” for new characters or items. The rarity of these items varies, with the most powerful and sought-after characters being incredibly difficult to obtain. For the completionist, this presents a tantalizing challenge, a mountain to be climbed, no matter the cost. The result is a multi-billion dollar industry built on the back of the collector’s compulsion.

But the completionist’s spending is not limited to the virtual realm. The rise of collector’s editions of video games is another testament to their willingness to spend more. These premium-priced packages often include a variety of physical goodies, such as art books, statues, and soundtracks, in addition to the game itself. For the completionist, the collector’s edition is the only way to truly “own” the game, to have the complete and definitive version. The fact that these editions often sell out within minutes of being announced is a clear indication of the demand for these premium products. A particularly noteworthy example is the “Pip-Boy Edition” of *Fallout 4*, which included a life-sized replica of the in-game wrist-mounted computer. The item was so popular that it sold out almost immediately, and a second wave of production was quickly announced to meet the overwhelming demand. This willingness to spend a significant amount of money on a physical object that is, for all intents and purposes, a glorified phone case, is a powerful illustration of the completionist’s desire for tangible representations of their virtual achievements.

## DISCUSSION

The completionist, with their insatiable desire to collect and their willingness to spend, may seem like an anomaly, a niche group of consumers with a peculiar and expensive hobby. But to dismiss them as such would be to miss the larger point. The psychological drivers that motivate the completionist – the fear of missing out, the desire for control, the thrill of the hunt – are not unique to them. They are universal human traits, and they influence our consumer behavior in ways that we may not even realize.

Consider the loyalty card for your local coffee shop. Every time you buy a coffee, you get a stamp. Collect ten stamps, and you get a free coffee. For the completionist, this is a challenge to be conquered, a mini-game to be won. But even for the average consumer, there is a certain satisfaction in completing the card, a sense of accomplishment that goes beyond the simple pleasure of a free cup of coffee. It is a small, everyday example of the completionist impulse at work.

Or think about the last time you binge-watched a series on a streaming service. Did you feel a sense of satisfaction when you finished the last episode? A sense of closure? That, too, is a form of completionism. We are, it seems, a species that is hardwired to seek out closure, to tie up loose ends, to complete the set. And in a world of endless choice and constant stimulation, this desire for completion is stronger than ever.

The implications for consumer behavior are profound. As our lives become increasingly gamified, with points, badges, and leaderboards being integrated into everything from our fitness routines to our financial planning, the completionist impulse will only become more powerful. Businesses that understand this, that can tap into our innate desire to collect and complete, will be the ones that succeed in the 21st-century marketplace.

## CONCLUSION

The completionist, in all their obsessive, free-spending glory, is more than just a caricature of the modern gamer. They are a window into the human soul, a reflection of our deepest fears and desires. They are a reminder that, in a world of fleeting moments and ephemeral experiences, there is a powerful and enduring appeal in the act of collecting, of creating something permanent and complete.

So the next time you see a player bragging about their 100% completion status, or a collector proudly displaying their complete set of limited-edition figurines, do not be too quick to judge. For in their seemingly irrational behavior, you may just find a reflection of your own deepest desires. And who knows, you may even be tempted to start a collection of your own. Just be sure to keep an eye on your bank account.
references:
[1] Georgiou, G. (2021, July 15). *Fun & Grief: Completionism in Games*. Game Developer. Retrieved from https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/fun-grief-completionism-in-games
[2] Mueller, S. M. (2023, January 25). *Spending Money on Collectibles: A Battle Within?* Psychology Today. Retrieved from https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/the-mind-of-a-collector/202301/spending-money-on-collectibles-a-battle-within
